title: Financial Management
summary: Financial Management is concerned with planning, raising, controlling, and administering funds used in a business.
Three financial decisions: Investment Decision (where to invest), Financing Decision (how to raise funds — debt or equity), Dividend Decision (how much profit to distribute).
Capital Structure = Debt-Equity ratio. More debt → higher risk but higher EPS (due to tax savings on interest). The ideal mix depends on cost of capital, risk, flexibility, and control.
Financial Planning = estimating the funds required and determining their sources. Objectives: ensure adequate funds, avoid idle surplus, maintain flexibility.
Fixed Capital: Long-term assets (land, machinery). Factors: nature of business, scale, technology, growth prospects.
Working Capital: Short-term assets needed for day-to-day operations (inventory, cash, receivables). Shorter operating cycle = less working capital needed.

teacher_tip.title: AKSpected Shortcut
teacher_tip.text: If the question says 'which project to invest in' → Investment decision. 'Debt or equity' → Financing decision. 'How much dividend to pay' → Dividend decision. 'Land and machinery' → Fixed capital. 'Day-to-day expenses, inventory, cash' → Working capital. Remember: Debt is cheaper (tax-deductible interest) but risky; Equity is safer but dilutes control.

sections.title: Financial Decisions
points: 1. Investment (Capital Budgeting) Decision: Determining where to invest long-term funds. Factors: cash flow, rate of return, risk involved, investment criteria.
2. Financing Decision: Determining the best mix of debt and equity. Debt: cheaper (interest is tax-deductible) but increases financial risk. Equity: no fixed obligation but dilutes ownership.

3. Dividend Decision: How much of the net profit to distribute as dividends vs retain for reinvestment. Factors: earnings stability, cash flow, growth opportunities, shareholder expectations, taxation.

sections.title: Capital Structure
sections.content: Capital structure refers to the mix of debt (borrowed funds) and equity (owner's funds) used to finance a firm.
points: • Cost of Capital: Cheaper source should be preferred, overall cost should be minimised.
• Risk: Higher debt = higher financial risk (fixed interest obligation).
• Cash Flow: Steady cash flows support more debt.
• Flexibility: Capital structure should allow room for future borrowing.
• Tax Rate: Higher tax rate makes debt more attractive (interest is deductible).
• Stock Market Conditions: Bullish market → issue equity. Bearish → take debt.

sections.title: Financial Planning

definition.text: The process of estimating the fund requirements of a business and determining the sources of funds.

points: Objectives: Ensure funds are available when needed, avoid idle surplus, maintain flexibility.
Twin objectives: (1) Adequate funds must be available (2) Firm should not raise unnecessary resources.

sections.title: Fixed and Working Capital
points: Fixed Capital: Investment in long-term assets like land, building, machinery, equipment.
Factors: Nature of business, Scale of operations, Choice of technology, Growth and diversification, Financing alternatives available.
Working Capital: Funds needed for day-to-day operations — raw materials, wages, bills, inventory.
Factors: Nature of business, Scale of operations, Business cycle (boom needs more), Seasonal factors, Production cycle length, Credit terms (allowed and availed).
Operating Cycle: Cash → Raw Material → Work-in-Progress → Finished Goods → Sales → Receivables → Cash. Shorter cycle = less working capital needed.

tables.title: Debt vs Equity
tables.tip: Fundamental concept for financing decisions. Know all differences.
rows: When risk is high or market is bullish

questions.text: Interest on debt is tax-deductible. This makes debt:
questions.answer: Cheaper than equity as a source of finance — the tax shield reduces the effective cost of borrowing.

questions.text: Explain any three factors affecting the financing decision.
questions.answer:
(i) Cost: Cheaper sources should be preferred to minimise overall cost of capital.
(ii) Risk: More debt increases financial risk due to fixed interest obligations.
(iii) Control: Debt doesn't dilute ownership; equity does — owners wanting to retain control prefer debt.

questions.text: State any three factors affecting working capital requirements.
questions.answer:
(i) Nature of Business: Trading firms need more working capital than service firms.
(ii) Business Cycle: Boom period requires more working capital due to higher demand.
(iii) Production Cycle: Longer production cycles lock funds in work-in-progress for longer.

pyqs.text: Explain any four factors affecting the capital structure of a company.
pyqs.answer:
(1) Cost of Capital: Minimise overall cost by choosing the cheapest mix.
(2) Risk: Higher debt means higher financial risk.
(3) Cash Flow: Stable cash flows can support more debt.
(4) Tax Rate: Higher tax rates make debt more attractive due to interest deductibility.

related_chapters.title: Financial Markets
related_chapters.reason: Markets provide the platforms for raising funds

related_chapters.title: Planning
related_chapters.reason: Financial planning is a specific type of planning
